SUMMARY We have analyzed every publicly disclosed equity round raised by pure-play vertical AI companies between August 2025 and July 2026, a 12-month window covering every geography. We only kept rounds of $300K or more, excluded undisclosed amounts, and focused on companies where more than 80% of activity is dedicated to one industry-specific AI workflow. Over this period, fundraising in the vertical AI market was broad and active. The dataset includes 73 disclosed deals, 72 unique companies, and $3.07B raised. The vertical AI market is not dominated by deal count in a single category. Legal AI, Insurance AI, Construction AI, and Healthcare AI together account for 72.64% of disclosed capital while representing 57.53% of disclosed deals. Capital is concentrated, but not as extremely as in some deep-tech markets. The top deal represents 8.78% of total capital, the top 3 deals reach 21.80%, and the top 10 reach 50.82%. The median round size is $22M, while the average round size is $42.1M. That gap shows large rounds materially lift the headline funding number. Deal flow averaged 6.08 rounds per month, with the densest formation window in January, February, and March 2026. Those three months produced 38 of the 73 disclosed deals. Legal AI leads the vertical AI market by capital, with $604.3M raised. Healthcare AI leads by deal count, with 12 disclosed rounds. North America dominates the vertical AI market. It captures 93.2% of disclosed capital and 87.67% of deals, while Europe and Asia-Pacific remain visible but much smaller. The vertical AI market is still centered on Series A. Series A rounds represent 56.16% of deals and 35.8% of capital, which points to a market still scaling product-market fit. Late-stage rounds still matter. Series B and later rounds account for 60.37% of disclosed capital, showing that some vertical AI companies have already crossed from experimentation into scale financing. Repeat investor appearances are clustered around legal, construction, finance, and insurance. That suggests investors are beginning to build exposure across adjacent regulated workflows rather than backing only isolated applications.

OUR METHODOLOGY TO BUILD THIS TRACKER We built this vertical AI funding tracker by reviewing every publicly disclosed equity round raised by pure-play vertical AI companies between August 2025 and July 2026. A company counts as pure-play when more than 80% of its activity is dedicated to one industry-specific AI workflow, dataset, buyer, or economic problem. We applied four filters to build the dataset. First, we only included equity rounds, so grants, debt, and revenue financing are excluded. Second, we only counted rounds of $300K or more. Third, we only kept pure-play vertical AI companies. And fourth, every entry had to be confirmed by a direct company announcement, a press release, or a tier-1 media report, with the source URL preserved for every row. The final dataset contains 73 disclosed deals across 72 unique companies, and every average, median, share, and concentration ratio is computed on that disclosed sample. Privately raised rounds that were never publicly announced are necessarily missing, which is a known limitation of any public-only vertical AI funding tracker. How active has fundraising been in the vertical AI market? As of July 2026, fundraising in the vertical AI market has been very active across the past 12 months. The dataset includes 73 disclosed equity rounds and $3.07B raised across 72 unique companies. That activity works out to 6.08 deals per month on average, with a median of 4.5 deals per month. The vertical AI market therefore shows steady formation, not just a few isolated financing events. The dollar flow is also meaningful, with $256.1M raised per month on average. The median monthly capital figure is $246.2M, which means the market had several months with substantial funding rather than only one spike. The most active formation period came from January to March 2026. Those three months produced 38 of the 73 deals, which suggests fundraising accelerated after late-2025 validation. How concentrated has fundraising been in the vertical AI market? As of July 2026, fundraising in the vertical AI market is moderately concentrated, but not completely dominated by one company. Over the past 12 months, the top deal accounts for 8.78% of capital, the top 3 reach 21.80%, and the top 10 reach 50.82%. This means half of the disclosed capital is still explained by the ten largest rounds. Total funding headlines in the vertical AI market therefore describe the top decile more than the full company base. At the category level, concentration is clearer. Legal AI, Insurance AI, Construction AI, and Healthcare AI together represent 72.64% of capital while accounting for 57.53% of deals. The right reading is that vertical AI is broad in company count but selective in capital allocation. Investors are writing premium checks only where workflows look regulated, expensive, repeatable, and budgeted. How much of the vertical AI funding signal is driven by outliers? As of July 2026, outliers drive a large share of the vertical AI funding signal, but they do not erase the broader market. Over the past 12 months, 17 deals were $50M or larger, and those rounds represented most of the dollars. Rounds above $50M account for about $1.875B of capital, or roughly 61% of the total, despite representing only 17 of 73 deals. That makes the vertical AI market sensitive to a small number of conviction events. Removing rounds above $50M reduces total capital from $3.07B to $1.20B. The remaining market is still active, but its capital base looks much more like a broad Series A ecosystem. The median round size of $22M is therefore more useful than the average of $42.1M when judging a typical deal. The average is pulled upward by scale rounds in categories like construction, legal, insurance, retail, and healthcare.
Is the vertical AI market broad with many targets, or narrow with few fundable companies? As of July 2026, the vertical AI market is broad by company count but narrower by scale-capital access. Over the past 12 months, 72 unique companies raised 73 disclosed rounds, so the dataset is not built around a small repeat-raiser group. Deal count is spread across many categories. Healthcare AI has 12 deals, while Legal AI, Insurance AI, and Retail AI each have 11 deals. Finance AI adds 10 more, which confirms broad company formation. Capital allocation is less evenly spread. Legal AI, Insurance AI, Construction AI, and Healthcare AI capture nearly three-quarters of all disclosed dollars, leaving the rest of the market with smaller checks. This means the vertical AI market is broad at the seed and Series A layer, but more selective at the scale layer. Many targets are fundable, but fewer are being underwritten as large category winners. Is vertical AI mostly an early-stage formation market or a late-stage scaling market? As of July 2026, the vertical AI market is mostly a Series A formation market with a meaningful late-stage scaling layer. Over the past 12 months, Seed and Series A rounds represented 75.34% of deals, while Series B and later rounds represented 60.37% of capital. Series A is the center of gravity by deal count. It accounts for 41 of 73 rounds, or 56.16% of the dataset, which suggests many companies are still proving go-to-market repeatability. Seed rounds are visible but not capital-heavy. They represent 19.18% of deals but only 3.83% of capital, which means investors are funding new company formation without assigning large checks yet. The late-stage capital share matters because it shows vertical AI is not purely experimental. Series B and later rounds attracted $1.86B, which suggests some companies have moved beyond pilots into scaling workflows. Which categories attract the most investor attention in vertical AI? As of July 2026, Healthcare AI attracts the most investor attention by deal count, while Legal AI attracts the most capital in the vertical AI market. Over the past 12 months, Healthcare AI produced 12 deals, and Legal AI raised $604.3M. Legal AI, Insurance AI, Construction AI, and Healthcare AI are the four largest capital categories. Together they account for 72.64% of all disclosed funding, which shows investors prefer regulated and operationally complex verticals. Healthcare AI looks broad rather than outlier-led. It has the most deals and a capital share-to-deal share ratio of 0.99, making it the most balanced category in the dataset. Legal AI has a stronger capital signal, but Harvey heavily influences that position. Harvey appears twice and contributes $360M, so the category is diversified underneath a very visible leader.
Which categories attract disproportionately large checks in the vertical AI market? As of July 2026, Construction AI attracts the most disproportionately large checks in the vertical AI market. Over the past 12 months, it represented 10.96% of deals but 17.65% of capital, giving it the highest capital-to-deal ratio at 1.61x. Construction AI also has the highest average deal size at $67.8M. Bedrock Robotics, Higharc, PermitFlow, Bobyard, and Attentive.ai show that investors will fund both physical automation and workflow software when the economic bottleneck is clear. Legal AI and Insurance AI also attract above-average check intensity. Their capital-to-deal ratios are 1.30x and 1.26x, which confirms that regulated workflows can support larger financing rounds. Education AI sits at the other end of the market. It has 4.11% of deals but only 1.25% of capital, suggesting investors remain cautious about education-specific monetization, procurement, and defensibility. Which geographies matter most for fundraising in the vertical AI market? As of July 2026, North America is by far the most important geography for fundraising in the vertical AI market. Over the past 12 months, it captured $2.86B, or 93.2% of disclosed capital. North America also leads by deal count, with 64 of the 73 disclosed rounds. Its average deal size is $44.8M, and its median round is $22.5M. Europe is visible but much smaller. It produced 6 deals and $165.2M, with a median deal size of $21M, which is close to North America’s median but across far fewer companies. Asia-Pacific is present but not yet scaling in this dataset. It produced 3 deals and $43.9M, with a median round of $7.2M, far below the North American and European medians.
Is vertical AI a market of small experiments or scaled financings? As of July 2026, the vertical AI market is a mix of serious Series A formation and scaled financings. Over the past 12 months, the median round was $22M, and 17 disclosed deals were $50M or larger. The most common deal-size bucket is $20M to less than $50M, with 28 deals. That bucket is large enough to show institutional backing, but not so large that every company must already be a category leader. The lower-middle layer is also healthy. There were 24 deals between $5M and less than $20M, which confirms that vertical AI is not only a mega-round market. At the same time, the largest rounds shape the dollar story. Deals above $50M represent only 23.29% of deal count but about 61% of capital, so scaled financings define headline momentum. Who are the investors that appear the most in vertical AI fundraising? As of July 2026, repeat investors in the vertical AI market are clustered around a small group of high-conviction software and platform funds. Over the past 12 months, 8VC, Andreessen Horowitz, Insight Partners, and Y Combinator each appeared in multiple disclosed investor lists. 8VC appeared across Mendra, Bedrock Robotics, and Bobyard. That gives it exposure to healthcare AI and construction AI, two categories where workflow complexity and operational pain are central to the thesis. Andreessen Horowitz appeared in Smart Bricks, XBuild, and Harvey. Those deals span real estate, construction, and legal AI, which suggests a focus on vertical workflow systems rather than one narrow category. Insight Partners appeared in Attentive.ai, Pasito, and Higharc. Y Combinator appeared in Model ML, Sphinx, Pasito, and Harper, showing repeat exposure across finance AI and insurance AI. One important caveat is that round announcements usually disclose participants, not individual check sizes. So investor frequency is a better signal than dollars by investor in the vertical AI market.

Healthcare AI has 12 deals, while Legal AI, Insurance AI, and Retail AI each have 11 deals. Finance AI adds 10 more, which confirms broad company formation. Capital allocation is less evenly spread. Legal AI, Insurance AI, Construction AI, and Healthcare AI capture nearly three-quarters of all disclosed dollars, leaving the rest of the market with smaller checks. This means the vertical AI market is broad at the seed and Series A layer, but more selective at the scale layer. Many targets are fundable, but fewer are being underwritten as large category winners. Is vertical AI mostly an early-stage formation market or a late-stage scaling market? As of July 2026, the vertical AI market is mostly a Series A formation market with a meaningful late-stage scaling layer. Over the past 12 months, Seed and Series A rounds represented 75.34% of deals, while Series B and later rounds represented 60.37% of capital. Series A is the center of gravity by deal count. It accounts for 41 of 73 rounds, or 56.16% of the dataset, which suggests many companies are still proving go-to-market repeatability. Seed rounds are visible but not capital-heavy. They represent 19.18% of deals but only 3.83% of capital, which means investors are funding new company formation without assigning large checks yet. The late-stage capital share matters because it shows vertical AI is not purely experimental. Series B and later rounds attracted $1.86B, which suggests some companies have moved beyond pilots into scaling workflows. Which categories attract the most investor attention in vertical AI? As of July 2026, Healthcare AI attracts the most investor attention by deal count, while Legal AI attracts the most capital in the vertical AI market. Over the past 12 months, Healthcare AI produced 12 deals, and Legal AI raised $604.3M. Legal AI, Insurance AI, Construction AI, and Healthcare AI are the four largest capital categories. Together they account for 72.64% of all disclosed funding, which shows investors prefer regulated and operationally complex verticals. Healthcare AI looks broad rather than outlier-led. It has the most deals and a capital share-to-deal share ratio of 0.99, making it the most balanced category in the dataset. Legal AI has a stronger capital signal, but Harvey heavily influences that position. Harvey appears twice and contributes $360M, so the category is diversified underneath a very visible leader.
Which categories attract disproportionately large checks in the vertical AI market? As of July 2026, Construction AI attracts the most disproportionately large checks in the vertical AI market. Over the past 12 months, it represented 10.96% of deals but 17.65% of capital, giving it the highest capital-to-deal ratio at 1.61x. Construction AI also has the highest average deal size at $67.8M. Bedrock Robotics, Higharc, PermitFlow, Bobyard, and Attentive.ai show that investors will fund both physical automation and workflow software when the economic bottleneck is clear. Legal AI and Insurance AI also attract above-average check intensity. Their capital-to-deal ratios are 1.30x and 1.26x, which confirms that regulated workflows can support larger financing rounds. Education AI sits at the other end of the market. It has 4.11% of deals but only 1.25% of capital, suggesting investors remain cautious about education-specific monetization, procurement, and defensibility. Which geographies matter most for fundraising in the vertical AI market? As of July 2026, North America is by far the most important geography for fundraising in the vertical AI market. Over the past 12 months, it captured $2.86B, or 93.2% of disclosed capital. North America also leads by deal count, with 64 of the 73 disclosed rounds. Its average deal size is $44.8M, and its median round is $22.5M. Europe is visible but much smaller. It produced 6 deals and $165.2M, with a median deal size of $21M, which is close to North America’s median but across far fewer companies. Asia-Pacific is present but not yet scaling in this dataset. It produced 3 deals and $43.9M, with a median round of $7.2M, far below the North American and European medians.
Is vertical AI a market of small experiments or scaled financings? As of July 2026, the vertical AI market is a mix of serious Series A formation and scaled financings. Over the past 12 months, the median round was $22M, and 17 disclosed deals were $50M or larger. The most common deal-size bucket is $20M to less than $50M, with 28 deals. That bucket is large enough to show institutional backing, but not so large that every company must already be a category leader. The lower-middle layer is also healthy. There were 24 deals between $5M and less than $20M, which confirms that vertical AI is not only a mega-round market. At the same time, the largest rounds shape the dollar story. Deals above $50M represent only 23.29% of deal count but about 61% of capital, so scaled financings define headline momentum. Who are the investors that appear the most in vertical AI fundraising? As of July 2026, repeat investors in the vertical AI market are clustered around a small group of high-conviction software and platform funds. Over the past 12 months, 8VC, Andreessen Horowitz, Insight Partners, and Y Combinator each appeared in multiple disclosed investor lists. 8VC appeared across Mendra, Bedrock Robotics, and Bobyard. That gives it exposure to healthcare AI and construction AI, two categories where workflow complexity and operational pain are central to the thesis. Andreessen Horowitz appeared in Smart Bricks, XBuild, and Harvey. Those deals span real estate, construction, and legal AI, which suggests a focus on vertical workflow systems rather than one narrow category. Insight Partners appeared in Attentive.ai, Pasito, and Higharc. Y Combinator appeared in Model ML, Sphinx, Pasito, and Harper, showing repeat exposure across finance AI and insurance AI. One important caveat is that round announcements usually disclose participants, not individual check sizes. So investor frequency is a better signal than dollars by investor in the vertical AI market.

OUR METHODOLOGY TO BUILD THIS TRACKER We built this vertical AI funding tracker by reviewing every publicly disclosed equity round raised by pure-play vertical AI companies between August 2025 and July 2026. A company counts as pure-play when more than 80% of its activity is dedicated to one industry-specific AI workflow, dataset, buyer, or economic problem. We applied four filters to build the dataset. First, we only included equity rounds, so grants, debt, and revenue financing are excluded. Second, we only counted rounds of $300K or more. Third, we only kept pure-play vertical AI companies. And fourth, every entry had to be confirmed by a direct company announcement, a press release, or a tier-1 media report, with the source URL preserved for every row. The final dataset contains 73 disclosed deals across 72 unique companies, and every average, median, share, and concentration ratio is computed on that disclosed sample. Privately raised rounds that were never publicly announced are necessarily missing, which is a known limitation of any public-only vertical AI funding tracker. How active has fundraising been in the vertical AI market? As of July 2026, fundraising in the vertical AI market has been very active across the past 12 months. The dataset includes 73 disclosed equity rounds and $3.07B raised across 72 unique companies. That activity works out to 6.08 deals per month on average, with a median of 4.5 deals per month. The vertical AI market therefore shows steady formation, not just a few isolated financing events. The dollar flow is also meaningful, with $256.1M raised per month on average. The median monthly capital figure is $246.2M, which means the market had several months with substantial funding rather than only one spike. The most active formation period came from January to March 2026. Those three months produced 38 of the 73 deals, which suggests fundraising accelerated after late-2025 validation. How concentrated has fundraising been in the vertical AI market? As of July 2026, fundraising in the vertical AI market is moderately concentrated, but not completely dominated by one company. Over the past 12 months, the top deal accounts for 8.78% of capital, the top 3 reach 21.80%, and the top 10 reach 50.82%. This means half of the disclosed capital is still explained by the ten largest rounds. Total funding headlines in the vertical AI market therefore describe the top decile more than the full company base. At the category level, concentration is clearer. Legal AI, Insurance AI, Construction AI, and Healthcare AI together represent 72.64% of capital while accounting for 57.53% of deals. The right reading is that vertical AI is broad in company count but selective in capital allocation. Investors are writing premium checks only where workflows look regulated, expensive, repeatable, and budgeted. How much of the vertical AI funding signal is driven by outliers? As of July 2026, outliers drive a large share of the vertical AI funding signal, but they do not erase the broader market. Over the past 12 months, 17 deals were $50M or larger, and those rounds represented most of the dollars. Rounds above $50M account for about $1.875B of capital, or roughly 61% of the total, despite representing only 17 of 73 deals. That makes the vertical AI market sensitive to a small number of conviction events. Removing rounds above $50M reduces total capital from $3.07B to $1.20B. The remaining market is still active, but its capital base looks much more like a broad Series A ecosystem. The median round size of $22M is therefore more useful than the average of $42.1M when judging a typical deal. The average is pulled upward by scale rounds in categories like construction, legal, insurance, retail, and healthcare.
Is the vertical AI market broad with many targets, or narrow with few fundable companies? As of July 2026, the vertical AI market is broad by company count but narrower by scale-capital access. Over the past 12 months, 72 unique companies raised 73 disclosed rounds, so the dataset is not built around a small repeat-raiser group. Deal count is spread across many categories. Healthcare AI has 12 deals, while Legal AI, Insurance AI, and Retail AI each have 11 deals. Finance AI adds 10 more, which confirms broad company formation. Capital allocation is less evenly spread. Legal AI, Insurance AI, Construction AI, and Healthcare AI capture nearly three-quarters of all disclosed dollars, leaving the rest of the market with smaller checks. This means the vertical AI market is broad at the seed and Series A layer, but more selective at the scale layer. Many targets are fundable, but fewer are being underwritten as large category winners. Is vertical AI mostly an early-stage formation market or a late-stage scaling market? As of July 2026, the vertical AI market is mostly a Series A formation market with a meaningful late-stage scaling layer. Over the past 12 months, Seed and Series A rounds represented 75.34% of deals, while Series B and later rounds represented 60.37% of capital. Series A is the center of gravity by deal count. It accounts for 41 of 73 rounds, or 56.16% of the dataset, which suggests many companies are still proving go-to-market repeatability. Seed rounds are visible but not capital-heavy. They represent 19.18% of deals but only 3.83% of capital, which means investors are funding new company formation without assigning large checks yet. The late-stage capital share matters because it shows vertical AI is not purely experimental. Series B and later rounds attracted $1.86B, which suggests some companies have moved beyond pilots into scaling workflows. Which categories attract the most investor attention in vertical AI? As of July 2026, Healthcare AI attracts the most investor attention by deal count, while Legal AI attracts the most capital in the vertical AI market. Over the past 12 months, Healthcare AI produced 12 deals, and Legal AI raised $604.3M. Legal AI, Insurance AI, Construction AI, and Healthcare AI are the four largest capital categories. Together they account for 72.64% of all disclosed funding, which shows investors prefer regulated and operationally complex verticals. Healthcare AI looks broad rather than outlier-led. It has the most deals and a capital share-to-deal share ratio of 0.99, making it the most balanced category in the dataset. Legal AI has a stronger capital signal, but Harvey heavily influences that position. Harvey appears twice and contributes $360M, so the category is diversified underneath a very visible leader.
Which categories attract disproportionately large checks in the vertical AI market? As of July 2026, Construction AI attracts the most disproportionately large checks in the vertical AI market. Over the past 12 months, it represented 10.96% of deals but 17.65% of capital, giving it the highest capital-to-deal ratio at 1.61x. Construction AI also has the highest average deal size at $67.8M. Bedrock Robotics, Higharc, PermitFlow, Bobyard, and Attentive.ai show that investors will fund both physical automation and workflow software when the economic bottleneck is clear. Legal AI and Insurance AI also attract above-average check intensity. Their capital-to-deal ratios are 1.30x and 1.26x, which confirms that regulated workflows can support larger financing rounds. Education AI sits at the other end of the market. It has 4.11% of deals but only 1.25% of capital, suggesting investors remain cautious about education-specific monetization, procurement, and defensibility. Which geographies matter most for fundraising in the vertical AI market? As of July 2026, North America is by far the most important geography for fundraising in the vertical AI market. Over the past 12 months, it captured $2.86B, or 93.2% of disclosed capital. North America also leads by deal count, with 64 of the 73 disclosed rounds. Its average deal size is $44.8M, and its median round is $22.5M. Europe is visible but much smaller. It produced 6 deals and $165.2M, with a median deal size of $21M, which is close to North America’s median but across far fewer companies. Asia-Pacific is present but not yet scaling in this dataset. It produced 3 deals and $43.9M, with a median round of $7.2M, far below the North American and European medians.
Is vertical AI a market of small experiments or scaled financings? As of July 2026, the vertical AI market is a mix of serious Series A formation and scaled financings. Over the past 12 months, the median round was $22M, and 17 disclosed deals were $50M or larger. The most common deal-size bucket is $20M to less than $50M, with 28 deals. That bucket is large enough to show institutional backing, but not so large that every company must already be a category leader. The lower-middle layer is also healthy. There were 24 deals between $5M and less than $20M, which confirms that vertical AI is not only a mega-round market. At the same time, the largest rounds shape the dollar story. Deals above $50M represent only 23.29% of deal count but about 61% of capital, so scaled financings define headline momentum. Who are the investors that appear the most in vertical AI fundraising? As of July 2026, repeat investors in the vertical AI market are clustered around a small group of high-conviction software and platform funds. Over the past 12 months, 8VC, Andreessen Horowitz, Insight Partners, and Y Combinator each appeared in multiple disclosed investor lists. 8VC appeared across Mendra, Bedrock Robotics, and Bobyard. That gives it exposure to healthcare AI and construction AI, two categories where workflow complexity and operational pain are central to the thesis. Andreessen Horowitz appeared in Smart Bricks, XBuild, and Harvey. Those deals span real estate, construction, and legal AI, which suggests a focus on vertical workflow systems rather than one narrow category. Insight Partners appeared in Attentive.ai, Pasito, and Higharc. Y Combinator appeared in Model ML, Sphinx, Pasito, and Harper, showing repeat exposure across finance AI and insurance AI. One important caveat is that round announcements usually disclose participants, not individual check sizes. So investor frequency is a better signal than dollars by investor in the vertical AI market.
